FourGate Decision System · 2026

Four Gates.
One farm.
One decision.

Every cultivation decision starts with one question:
What should I grow, and will it actually survive reality?

FourGate takes your farm conditions through four structured gates — soil to market — and returns a clear viability signal before you commit capital, land, or water.

Muhammad Uzair Ilyas · Agricultural Planning Framework
Works for any crop, any country, any scale.
Complete all sections honestly. The verdict is only as good as your inputs.
Gate 1

What Should I Grow?

01

Where is your farm located?

Include country, region, and approximate elevation or climate zone if known. These determine which crops are realistic options.

02

What is your total cultivable land area?

Use whatever unit is standard in your country — acres, hectares, bigha, feddan, mu, etc.

03

What crops do other farmers in your area commonly grow?

Locally grown crops signal proven agro-climatic fit. List what you see around you — this is your baseline.

04

What is your primary water source?

05

How many irrigations can you reliably deliver per crop cycle?

This determines which crops are viable. A crop that needs 8 waterings on a farm that can manage 3 will underperform structurally, not just in a bad year.

06

What capital can you commit to this cultivation cycle?

Write the amount in your local currency. Include all costs you expect: seed, inputs, labour, land rent, transport.

Amount
Currency
07

What crop are you planning to grow?

If undecided, list 2–3 options. This framework will help you identify which one survives your actual conditions.

Gate 2

How Do I Grow It Cheaply?

08

What is your soil type and current fertility condition?

09

Have you done a soil test in the last two years?

Soil tests tell you what nutrients to add and which to skip — cutting input costs while protecting yield. Skipping them is one of the most expensive guesses in farming.

10

Estimate your total input cost per unit of land for this crop

Include seed, fertiliser, pest and disease control, labour (hired and family), irrigation, land preparation, and transport to market. Use the same land unit as Q02. Be honest — underestimating inputs is the most common planning error.

Cost per unit
Currency / unit
11

Do you own or rent the land?

12

What is your primary cost advantage on this farm?

What lets you produce cheaper than average? Own equipment, access to subsidised inputs, family labour, on-farm organic matter — anything that reduces your per-unit cost.

Gate 3

What Can Go Wrong?

13

What yield are you expecting per unit of land?

Use the realistic average for your region and conditions — not the best-case figure from a seed company or a neighbour's exceptional year.

Expected yield
Unit
14

What market price are you expecting at harvest?

Expected price
Currency / unit
15

If the market price drops 25% at harvest, do you still break even?

Harvest gluts, currency shifts, and policy changes regularly push prices 20–30% below planting-season expectations. A plan that only works at peak price is not a plan.

16

What is the main crop disease, pest, or climate risk in your region for this crop?

17

How much financial risk can you absorb if this season is poor?

18

If this crop fails entirely, what is your fallback?

Gate 4

How and Where Will I Sell?

19

Where will you sell your harvest?

20

Do you have a confirmed or likely buyer before planting?

21

Can you hold your crop in storage if prices are low at harvest?

Farmers who must sell immediately at harvest are price-takers. Storage — even basic, dry, pest-free storage — creates options.

22

Is there a government support price, subsidy, or insurance scheme for this crop in your country?

23

What is your plan for next season based on this crop's outcome?

Verdict

Viability Checklist

Check each condition that is true for your farm — based on your actual data, not your hopes.

Q01–03 — My farm location, land size, and regional crop context are clearly defined
Q04–05 — My water supply can meet this crop's irrigation requirements through the full season
Q06 — My available capital covers total estimated input costs with a buffer for surprises
Q08–10 — My soil conditions suit this crop and my per-unit input cost is realistically calculated
Q13–15 — I remain viable even if market price at harvest is 25% below my expectation
Q16–17 — I understand the main pest, disease, and weather risks and have a response plan
Q19–21 — I have a clear sales channel identified and the ability to hold stock if harvest prices are low
Q18 + Q22–23 — I have a fallback if this crop fails and a clear plan for the following season
0
of 8 conditions met
Verdict
Complete the checklist above.

Every agricultural season has three stages — planning, growing, and harvest.

Find a problem at planning: fix it by switching crops, adjusting inputs, or securing a better buyer.
Find it during the growing season: it costs money and reduces yield but can still be managed.
Find it at harvest: the loss is already done. Nothing is recoverable.

This framework exists for the planning stage.
Use it before you buy seed, hire labour, or commit water.
Planning is the only stage where failure is still cheap to correct.

This is an educational decision-making tool for agricultural planning and farm structuring. It does not constitute financial, agronomic, or legal advice. The verdict is generated strictly from user-entered data. All farming, investment, and operational decisions remain the sole responsibility of the user.
